title: Leicester Square Christmas Market, London, England
tags: christmas xmas uk england london night dark festive square fun holidays leicester seasonal fair gb
description:
Leicester Square i/ˈlɛstər/ is a pedestrianisedsquare in the West End of London, England. It was laid out in 1670 and is named after the contemporary Leicester House, itself named after Robert Sidney, 2nd Earl of Leicester.

The square was originally a gentrified residential area, with notable tenants including Frederick, Prince of Wales and artists William Hogarth and Joshua Reynolds. It became more down-market in the late 18th century as Leicester House was demolished and retail developments took place, becoming a centre for entertainment. Several major theatres were established in the 19th century, which eventually became converted to cinemas towards the mid-20th. Leicester Square holds a number of nationally important cinemas frequently used for film premières, including the Odeon Leicester Square, Empire, Leicester Square and Odeon West End, while the nearby Prince Charles Cinema is popular for showing cult films and marathon film runs. The square remains a popular tourist attraction, including hosting events for the Chinese New Year.

The square has always had a park in its centre, which was originally Lammas land. The park's fortunes have varied over the centuries, reaching near dilapidation in the mid-19th, but it has since been restored. The square was extensively refurbished and remodelled for the 2012 London Olympics.

The square lies within an area bound by Lisle Street, to the north; Charing Cross Road, to the east; Orange Street, to the south; and Whitcomb Street, to the west. The park at the centre of the Square is bound by Cranbourn Street, to the north; Leicester Street, to the east; Irving Street, to the south; and a section of road designated simply as Leicester Square, to the west. It is within the City of Westminster, and about equal distances north of Trafalgar Square, east of Piccadilly Circus, west of Covent Garden, and south of Cambridge Circus.

The nearest tube station is Leicester Square tube station, which opened in 1906. London bus routes 24, 29 and 176 run on nearby Charing Cross Road.

History

The land where Leicester Square now lies once belonged to the Abbot and Convent of Westminster Abbey and the Beaumont family. In 1536, Henry VIII took control of 3 acres (1.2 ha) of land around the square, with the remaining 4 acres (1.6 ha) being transferred to the king the following year. The Square is named after Robert Sidney, 2nd Earl of Leicester, who purchased this land in 1630. By 1635, he had built himself a large house, Leicester House, at the northern end. The area in front of the house was then enclosed, depriving inhabitants of St Martin in the Fieldsparish of their right to use the previously common land. The parishioners appealed to King Charles I, and he appointed three members of the privy council to arbitrate. Lord Leicester was ordered to keep part of his land (thereafter known as Leicester Field and later as Leicester Square) open for the parishioners.

The square was laid out to the south of Leicester House and developed in the 1670s. The area was originally entirely residential, with properties laid out in a similar style to nearby Pall Mall.[4] In 1687, the northern part of the square became part of the new parish of St Anne, Soho. Leicester House was once the residence of Frederick, Prince of Wales  while the poet Matthew Prior lived at what is now No. 21 around 1700 and artist William Hogarth lived at No 30 between 1733 and 1764, where he produced some of his best known works including Gin Lane.  The magistrate Thomas de Veil, later to found Bow Street Magistrates' Court, lived at No 40 between 1729 and 1737; this location is now the Odeon West End. The painter Joshua Reynolds lived at No 47 from 1760 until his death in 1792; this location is now Fanum House, once the Automobile Association head office.

At the end of the 17th century, Lord Leicester's heir, Philip Sidney, 3rd Earl of Leicester, permitted a small amount of retail development in booths along the front of Leicester House. A statue of King George I was built on the square in 1760 following the coronation of his grandson, George III. The square remained fashionable throughout most of the 18th century, with notable residents including the architect James Stuart at No 35 from 1766 to 1788 and the painter John Singleton Copley at No. 28 from 1776 to 1783. Towards the end of the century, the square stopped becoming a desirable address and began to serve as a venue for popular entertainments. Brothels started appearing around Leicester Square during the century, and visitors could pay to watch the severed heads of traitors executed at Temple Bar through a telescope. Leicester House became home of a museum of natural curiosities called the Holophusikon in the 1780s. It was demolished in 1791–72 due to rising debts following the extinction of the Leicester peerage, and replaced by Leicester Place. That in turn was converted into a church in 1865 and is now the site of the Prince Charles Cinema.

In 1790, a new Royal Opera House was proposed to be built in Leicester Square. The scheme was led by The Prince of Wales, Francis Russell, 5th Duke of Bedford and James Cecil, 1st Marquess of Salisbury and aimed to re-establish London as a centre for Italian opera and ballet, with an opera house to rival those in mainland Europe. The opera house was never built, as the royal patent, needed at that time to license a theatre, was refused. The plans for the original design are preserved in Sir John Soane's Museum and a 1790 painting by William Hodges, showing the finished design, is part of the Museum of London's collection.


By the 19th century, Leicester Square was known as an entertainment venue, with many amusements peculiar to the era, including Wyld's Great Globe, which was built for The Great Exhibition of 1851 and housed a giant scale map of the Earth. The construction of New Coventry Street made it easier for traffic to access the square, resulting in private residences being replaced by shops, museums and exhibition centres. Savile House, built in 1683, had become a museum by this time. Several hotels were established around the square, making it popular with visitors to London. The Alhambra Theatre was built in 1854 on the east side of the square, dominating the site. It temporarily closed two years later when the original owner, Edward Clarke, became bankrupt, but then reopened in 1858 as the Alhambra Palace. It enjoyed a surge in popularity after Queen Victoria and family came to see "Black Eagle – The Horse of Beauty". It burned down in 1882, but reopened the following year. In the early 20th century, the theatre became a popular venue for ballet. It was demolished in 1936 and replaced by the Odeon Cinema. The Empire Theatre of Varieties opened in 1881 on the former site of Savile House, but had a troubled start, closing for a time, until the end of the decade. The theatre had a notorious reputation for high-class prostitutes frequenting the theatre, and in 1894 the London County Council ordered the promenade on the upper balcony to be remodelled. A young Winston Churchill, then a cadet at the Royal Military Academy Sandhurst, helped destroy canvas screens that had been erected to prevent access to the balcony. The theatre closed in 1927, to be replaced by the Empire Cinema.

During the Winter of Discontent, where the incumbent Labour Party struggled to meet demands of trade unions and a shrinking economy, refuse collectors went on strike in January 1979. Leicester Square was turned into a de facto dump, earning it the nickname of "Fester Square". In the 1980s, the square was pedestrianised, cutting off all vehicular traffic. Access to the square for goods and deliveries is now controlled by specially designated marshals.

By the start of the 21st century, Westminster City Council were concerned that the square was too dangerous at night, and wanted to demolish sections of it to accommodate more cafes, theatres and cinemas, and less nightclubs.  In 2010, a major redevelopment of Leicester Square took place as part of a Great Outdoors scheme proposed by the Mayor of London, Boris Johnson. The improvements included 12,000 square metres of granite paving and a water featuresurrounding the Shakespeare statue. The square re-opened in May 2012 after 17 months' work at a total cost of £15.3 million. The Greater London Authority said the refurbishments would accommodate more than 1,000 new jobs. The re-opening coincided with the 2012 Summer Olympics later that year.

Feature

In the middle of the Square is a small park. This was originally Lammas land that was available for common use on Lammas Day (12 August) for washing clothes and herding cattle. The Earl of Leicester was obliged to preserve these grounds, which were separated from the rest of the square with railings. In 1808, the garden was sold by the Leicester Estate into private ownership and subsequently deteriorated to the point of severe dilapidation by the mid-19th century.[6] In 1848, the land was subject to the significant legal case of Tulk v Moxhay. The plot's previous owner had agreed upon a covenant not to erect buildings but the law would not allow buyers who were not "privy" to the initial contract to be bound by subsequent promises. The judge, Lord Cottenham, decided that future owners could be bound by promises to abstain from activity. Otherwise, a buyer could sell land to himself to undermine an initial promise. When the Great Globe was erected, the statue of George I was buried under 12 foot of earth with the globe stuck on top. The statue was subsequently uncovered following the globe's demise, but by 1866 it had deteriorated due to vandalism and was sold for £16. Arguments continued about the fate of the garden, with Charles Augustus Tulk's heirs erecting a wooden hoarding around the property in 1873. These were quickly removed after the Master of the Rolls ordered that the land must be preserved for its original purpose.

The garden was saved by the Member of parliament Albert Grant, who purchased the park in 1874 for £11,060 and donated it to the Metropolitan Board of Works. The title deed for the square passed to the succeeding public bodies and is now in the ownership of the City of Westminster. After the purchase, the architect James Knowles redesigned the park. A statue of William Shakespeare surrounded by dolphins was constructed in the centre. The four corner gates of the park had one bust each of famous former residents in the square: the scientist Sir Isaac Newton; Sir Joshua Reynolds, the first President of the Royal Academy; John Hunter, a pioneer of surgery; and William Hogarth, the painter. The most recent addition was a statue of film star and director Charlie Chaplin in 1981. On the pavement were inscribed the distances in miles to several Commonwealth countries, including Canada, Kenya and Jamaica. After the Great Outdoors refurbishment of the square, only the statue of Shakespeare still remains.

EntertainmentEdit
Leicester Square is the centre of London's cinema land, and one of the signs marking the Square bears the legend "Theatreland". It contains the cinema with the largest screen and another with the most seats (over 1,600). The square is the prime location in London for film premières and co-hosts the London Film Festival each year. Similar to Grauman's Chinese Theatre in Hollywood, the square was surrounded by floor mounted plaques with film stars' names and cast handprints. During the 2010–2012 refurbishment, many of the plaques were removed, confusing tourists who still expected to find them there.

The Leicester Square Theatre is based in nearby Leicester Place. It was constructed in 1955 as a church, before becoming the Cavern in the Town, a popular live music venue in the 1960s and 70s. The Sex Pistols played one of their first gigs at the club. It was converted into a theatre in 2002 as The Venue, and refurbished as the Leicester Square Theatre in 2008. In 2014, it began a production of a musical based on Oscar Wilde's De Profundis.

The Square has been the home for TKTS, formerly known as the Official London Half-Price Theatre Ticket Booth, since 1980. Tickets for theatre performances taking place around the West End that day and during the week are sold from the booth at a significant discount. The popularity of the booth has given rise to other booths and stores around the Square that advertise half-price tickets for West End shows. The Official London Theatre Guide recommends avoiding these booths as they are not official and do not contain the Society of Ticket Agents & Retailers (STAR) logo.

The Square is home to the 93,000 square feet (8,600 m2) Hippodrome Casino. Following a £40m refurbishment in 2012, the premises can now accommodate 2,000 patrons.

Global Radio has its headquarters on the east side of Leicester Square at No. 30, close to the Odeon. The building houses the radio stations Capital, Capital Xtra, Classic FM, Gold, Heart, LBC, Smooth Radio and Radio X.


CinemasEdit
The Odeon Leicester Square, which dominates the east side of the square, hosts many film premières. It has a capacity for 1,683 people, arranged in circle and stalls.  The last 70mm film showing was Armageddon in 1998, after which the theatre began to use digital technology. The projection room still contains some of the original 1930s decor and normally houses two projectors. The Empire opened in 1962. It was previously the largest cinema on the square, but in 2013 it was subdivided to cater for an IMAX screen. The Odeon West End, on the south side, opened in 1930. It was not generally used for premières and was earmarked for demolition in 2014, to be replaced by a ten-storey hotel including a two-screen cinema. Westminster City Council reported 400 new jobs would be available after the redevelopment. Vue West End, on the north side, near the north east corner, was the first cinema in Europe to show a 3D film with Chicken Little in 2006.

A short distance from the west of the Square, on the south side of Panton Street, is the Odeon Panton Street. The Prince Charles Cinema, to the north of the square opened in 1962 with a "satellite dish" design where the audience looks upwards to the stage. The cinema became notorious for showing pornographic films during the 1970s, including Emmanuelle. It later became a favourite venue for showing cult films, including the The Rocky Horror Picture Show and a sing-along version of The Sound of Music, and marathon performances including all seven Muppet films back to back. Prices are considerably cheaper than the main cinemas in the square; in 2013 a ticket for a new release at the Prince Charles cost £10, compared to £24 at the Odeon.

title: Leicester Square Christmas Market, London, England
tags: christmas xmas uk england london night dark festive square fun holidays leicester seasonal fair gb
description:
Leicester Square i/ˈlɛstər/ is a pedestrianisedsquare in the West End of London, England. It was laid out in 1670 and is named after the contemporary Leicester House, itself named after Robert Sidney, 2nd Earl of Leicester.

The square was originally a gentrified residential area, with notable tenants including Frederick, Prince of Wales and artists William Hogarth and Joshua Reynolds. It became more down-market in the late 18th century as Leicester House was demolished and retail developments took place, becoming a centre for entertainment. Several major theatres were established in the 19th century, which eventually became converted to cinemas towards the mid-20th. Leicester Square holds a number of nationally important cinemas frequently used for film premières, including the Odeon Leicester Square, Empire, Leicester Square and Odeon West End, while the nearby Prince Charles Cinema is popular for showing cult films and marathon film runs. The square remains a popular tourist attraction, including hosting events for the Chinese New Year.

The square has always had a park in its centre, which was originally Lammas land. The park's fortunes have varied over the centuries, reaching near dilapidation in the mid-19th, but it has since been restored. The square was extensively refurbished and remodelled for the 2012 London Olympics.

The square lies within an area bound by Lisle Street, to the north; Charing Cross Road, to the east; Orange Street, to the south; and Whitcomb Street, to the west. The park at the centre of the Square is bound by Cranbourn Street, to the north; Leicester Street, to the east; Irving Street, to the south; and a section of road designated simply as Leicester Square, to the west. It is within the City of Westminster, and about equal distances north of Trafalgar Square, east of Piccadilly Circus, west of Covent Garden, and south of Cambridge Circus.

The nearest tube station is Leicester Square tube station, which opened in 1906. London bus routes 24, 29 and 176 run on nearby Charing Cross Road.

History

The land where Leicester Square now lies once belonged to the Abbot and Convent of Westminster Abbey and the Beaumont family. In 1536, Henry VIII took control of 3 acres (1.2 ha) of land around the square, with the remaining 4 acres (1.6 ha) being transferred to the king the following year. The Square is named after Robert Sidney, 2nd Earl of Leicester, who purchased this land in 1630. By 1635, he had built himself a large house, Leicester House, at the northern end. The area in front of the house was then enclosed, depriving inhabitants of St Martin in the Fieldsparish of their right to use the previously common land. The parishioners appealed to King Charles I, and he appointed three members of the privy council to arbitrate. Lord Leicester was ordered to keep part of his land (thereafter known as Leicester Field and later as Leicester Square) open for the parishioners.

The square was laid out to the south of Leicester House and developed in the 1670s. The area was originally entirely residential, with properties laid out in a similar style to nearby Pall Mall.[4] In 1687, the northern part of the square became part of the new parish of St Anne, Soho. Leicester House was once the residence of Frederick, Prince of Wales  while the poet Matthew Prior lived at what is now No. 21 around 1700 and artist William Hogarth lived at No 30 between 1733 and 1764, where he produced some of his best known works including Gin Lane.  The magistrate Thomas de Veil, later to found Bow Street Magistrates' Court, lived at No 40 between 1729 and 1737; this location is now the Odeon West End. The painter Joshua Reynolds lived at No 47 from 1760 until his death in 1792; this location is now Fanum House, once the Automobile Association head office.

At the end of the 17th century, Lord Leicester's heir, Philip Sidney, 3rd Earl of Leicester, permitted a small amount of retail development in booths along the front of Leicester House. A statue of King George I was built on the square in 1760 following the coronation of his grandson, George III. The square remained fashionable throughout most of the 18th century, with notable residents including the architect James Stuart at No 35 from 1766 to 1788 and the painter John Singleton Copley at No. 28 from 1776 to 1783. Towards the end of the century, the square stopped becoming a desirable address and began to serve as a venue for popular entertainments. Brothels started appearing around Leicester Square during the century, and visitors could pay to watch the severed heads of traitors executed at Temple Bar through a telescope. Leicester House became home of a museum of natural curiosities called the Holophusikon in the 1780s. It was demolished in 1791–72 due to rising debts following the extinction of the Leicester peerage, and replaced by Leicester Place. That in turn was converted into a church in 1865 and is now the site of the Prince Charles Cinema.

In 1790, a new Royal Opera House was proposed to be built in Leicester Square. The scheme was led by The Prince of Wales, Francis Russell, 5th Duke of Bedford and James Cecil, 1st Marquess of Salisbury and aimed to re-establish London as a centre for Italian opera and ballet, with an opera house to rival those in mainland Europe. The opera house was never built, as the royal patent, needed at that time to license a theatre, was refused. The plans for the original design are preserved in Sir John Soane's Museum and a 1790 painting by William Hodges, showing the finished design, is part of the Museum of London's collection.


By the 19th century, Leicester Square was known as an entertainment venue, with many amusements peculiar to the era, including Wyld's Great Globe, which was built for The Great Exhibition of 1851 and housed a giant scale map of the Earth. The construction of New Coventry Street made it easier for traffic to access the square, resulting in private residences being replaced by shops, museums and exhibition centres. Savile House, built in 1683, had become a museum by this time. Several hotels were established around the square, making it popular with visitors to London. The Alhambra Theatre was built in 1854 on the east side of the square, dominating the site. It temporarily closed two years later when the original owner, Edward Clarke, became bankrupt, but then reopened in 1858 as the Alhambra Palace. It enjoyed a surge in popularity after Queen Victoria and family came to see "Black Eagle – The Horse of Beauty". It burned down in 1882, but reopened the following year. In the early 20th century, the theatre became a popular venue for ballet. It was demolished in 1936 and replaced by the Odeon Cinema. The Empire Theatre of Varieties opened in 1881 on the former site of Savile House, but had a troubled start, closing for a time, until the end of the decade. The theatre had a notorious reputation for high-class prostitutes frequenting the theatre, and in 1894 the London County Council ordered the promenade on the upper balcony to be remodelled. A young Winston Churchill, then a cadet at the Royal Military Academy Sandhurst, helped destroy canvas screens that had been erected to prevent access to the balcony. The theatre closed in 1927, to be replaced by the Empire Cinema.

During the Winter of Discontent, where the incumbent Labour Party struggled to meet demands of trade unions and a shrinking economy, refuse collectors went on strike in January 1979. Leicester Square was turned into a de facto dump, earning it the nickname of "Fester Square". In the 1980s, the square was pedestrianised, cutting off all vehicular traffic. Access to the square for goods and deliveries is now controlled by specially designated marshals.

By the start of the 21st century, Westminster City Council were concerned that the square was too dangerous at night, and wanted to demolish sections of it to accommodate more cafes, theatres and cinemas, and less nightclubs.  In 2010, a major redevelopment of Leicester Square took place as part of a Great Outdoors scheme proposed by the Mayor of London, Boris Johnson. The improvements included 12,000 square metres of granite paving and a water featuresurrounding the Shakespeare statue. The square re-opened in May 2012 after 17 months' work at a total cost of £15.3 million. The Greater London Authority said the refurbishments would accommodate more than 1,000 new jobs. The re-opening coincided with the 2012 Summer Olympics later that year.

Feature

In the middle of the Square is a small park. This was originally Lammas land that was available for common use on Lammas Day (12 August) for washing clothes and herding cattle. The Earl of Leicester was obliged to preserve these grounds, which were separated from the rest of the square with railings. In 1808, the garden was sold by the Leicester Estate into private ownership and subsequently deteriorated to the point of severe dilapidation by the mid-19th century.[6] In 1848, the land was subject to the significant legal case of Tulk v Moxhay. The plot's previous owner had agreed upon a covenant not to erect buildings but the law would not allow buyers who were not "privy" to the initial contract to be bound by subsequent promises. The judge, Lord Cottenham, decided that future owners could be bound by promises to abstain from activity. Otherwise, a buyer could sell land to himself to undermine an initial promise. When the Great Globe was erected, the statue of George I was buried under 12 foot of earth with the globe stuck on top. The statue was subsequently uncovered following the globe's demise, but by 1866 it had deteriorated due to vandalism and was sold for £16. Arguments continued about the fate of the garden, with Charles Augustus Tulk's heirs erecting a wooden hoarding around the property in 1873. These were quickly removed after the Master of the Rolls ordered that the land must be preserved for its original purpose.

The garden was saved by the Member of parliament Albert Grant, who purchased the park in 1874 for £11,060 and donated it to the Metropolitan Board of Works. The title deed for the square passed to the succeeding public bodies and is now in the ownership of the City of Westminster. After the purchase, the architect James Knowles redesigned the park. A statue of William Shakespeare surrounded by dolphins was constructed in the centre. The four corner gates of the park had one bust each of famous former residents in the square: the scientist Sir Isaac Newton; Sir Joshua Reynolds, the first President of the Royal Academy; John Hunter, a pioneer of surgery; and William Hogarth, the painter. The most recent addition was a statue of film star and director Charlie Chaplin in 1981. On the pavement were inscribed the distances in miles to several Commonwealth countries, including Canada, Kenya and Jamaica. After the Great Outdoors refurbishment of the square, only the statue of Shakespeare still remains.

EntertainmentEdit
Leicester Square is the centre of London's cinema land, and one of the signs marking the Square bears the legend "Theatreland". It contains the cinema with the largest screen and another with the most seats (over 1,600). The square is the prime location in London for film premières and co-hosts the London Film Festival each year. Similar to Grauman's Chinese Theatre in Hollywood, the square was surrounded by floor mounted plaques with film stars' names and cast handprints. During the 2010–2012 refurbishment, many of the plaques were removed, confusing tourists who still expected to find them there.

The Leicester Square Theatre is based in nearby Leicester Place. It was constructed in 1955 as a church, before becoming the Cavern in the Town, a popular live music venue in the 1960s and 70s. The Sex Pistols played one of their first gigs at the club. It was converted into a theatre in 2002 as The Venue, and refurbished as the Leicester Square Theatre in 2008. In 2014, it began a production of a musical based on Oscar Wilde's De Profundis.

The Square has been the home for TKTS, formerly known as the Official London Half-Price Theatre Ticket Booth, since 1980. Tickets for theatre performances taking place around the West End that day and during the week are sold from the booth at a significant discount. The popularity of the booth has given rise to other booths and stores around the Square that advertise half-price tickets for West End shows. The Official London Theatre Guide recommends avoiding these booths as they are not official and do not contain the Society of Ticket Agents & Retailers (STAR) logo.

The Square is home to the 93,000 square feet (8,600 m2) Hippodrome Casino. Following a £40m refurbishment in 2012, the premises can now accommodate 2,000 patrons.

Global Radio has its headquarters on the east side of Leicester Square at No. 30, close to the Odeon. The building houses the radio stations Capital, Capital Xtra, Classic FM, Gold, Heart, LBC, Smooth Radio and Radio X.


CinemasEdit
The Odeon Leicester Square, which dominates the east side of the square, hosts many film premières. It has a capacity for 1,683 people, arranged in circle and stalls.  The last 70mm film showing was Armageddon in 1998, after which the theatre began to use digital technology. The projection room still contains some of the original 1930s decor and normally houses two projectors. The Empire opened in 1962. It was previously the largest cinema on the square, but in 2013 it was subdivided to cater for an IMAX screen. The Odeon West End, on the south side, opened in 1930. It was not generally used for premières and was earmarked for demolition in 2014, to be replaced by a ten-storey hotel including a two-screen cinema. Westminster City Council reported 400 new jobs would be available after the redevelopment. Vue West End, on the north side, near the north east corner, was the first cinema in Europe to show a 3D film with Chicken Little in 2006.

A short distance from the west of the Square, on the south side of Panton Street, is the Odeon Panton Street. The Prince Charles Cinema, to the north of the square opened in 1962 with a "satellite dish" design where the audience looks upwards to the stage. The cinema became notorious for showing pornographic films during the 1970s, including Emmanuelle. It later became a favourite venue for showing cult films, including the The Rocky Horror Picture Show and a sing-along version of The Sound of Music, and marathon performances including all seven Muppet films back to back. Prices are considerably cheaper than the main cinemas in the square; in 2013 a ticket for a new release at the Prince Charles cost £10, compared to £24 at the Odeon.

tags: barcelona greatbritain inglaterra england españa food fish london fountain valencia birds spain nikon chinatown cathedral market dom soho brunnen kathedrale catedral bahnhof ciudad fisch cathédrale mercado tapas pesci londres coolpix seafood angleterre d200 pescado cac markt poisson paella marché londra fuentes oiseau ville boqueria spanien vogel citta d800 inghilterra rambla mariscos ciudaddelasartesylasciencias d300 fruttidimare umbracle fruitsdemer meeresfrüchte oceanogràfic raiwaystation l’espagne bajaro estacióntrenes frankartculinaryyahoode
description:
Great Britain, London, Chinatown, Gerrard Street in London’s Soho is in the heart of Chinatown, it is in walking distance from Leicester Square, Piccadilly Circus & aprox. 500 mtr from Trafalgar Square. The Chinese community firmly established on Gerrard Street by the late 1960s, however, Chinatown goes back as far as the 18th century when it was in the East End. 

The London Chinatown has its own style, people feel displaced to Asia. A big gate marks the entrance to the Chinatowns Gerrad Street, here begins a pedestrian filled with red lanterns, dragons, Mandarin street signs & chock-a-block full of Chinese restaurants. The shops & eateries on the Gerrad Street are due to the tourists slightly more expensive than in the rest of Chinatown. It is particularly busy during the weekend, when the local Chinese community goes shopping for authentic Asian foodstuffs, not readily available elsewhere.

The most colourful time to visit Gerrard Street is during the Chinese New Year, at the end of January/early February, 2014 the year of the wooden horse is on January 31th, besides many activities, huge papier-mâché dragons dance through the streets with fire-crackers banging on the pavement.

The London Chinatown today occupies only a fraction of their former extent. During the ‘20s, Kate Meyrick opened up the notorious Club 43 on Gerrard Street, a decadent nightclub hosting outrageous parties frequented by the rich & famous. Famed London jazz institution Ronnie Scott’s first opened up at # 39 Gerrard Street in 1959, but for fans of rock music is London Chinatown incidentally also a term, a basement in Gerrard Street was the location of the first rehearsal of Led Zeppelin in August 1968, the well-known song "Train kept a rolling " was allegedly written here. Due to the many clubs existing in Soho in the past, it is considered as the birthplace of British Rock ’n’ Roll.

title: February 1, Chinese New Year
tags: london smile chinesenewyear celebrations photoaday laugh cultural photo365
description:
Although it's a Saturday, I still had to go into work.  Normally the walk to work is quiet, but today was busy because of the Chinese New Year celebrations.  I started to get quite annoyed because of the crowds, but then realised my daily photo opportunity was waiting for me within the throngs of people.

The crowds did make it difficult to get a decent photo of the dragon though, so I settled for a shot of the drummers getting wet. It seems someone gave the dragon some beer, who promptly squirted it back out over the people nearby.

Not the best photo I could have taken, but I was on my way to work so only took a moment. Still, I was happy with the smile I captured.

title: 20140205_F0001: The worshipers of the Chinese lion king
tags: street camera red people blackandwhite bw horse london festival children photography dance costume spring chinatown photographer phone candid traditional crowd chinese lion newyear celebration event acrobat tablet crowded selectivecolouring selectivecoloring
description: - This was one of the acts going around the Chinatown in London during the Chinese new year celebrations. It's a lion dance act, which is performed by two acrobats inside a traditional lion suit. Wherever it went, the large "clump" of people followed it, and it was almost impossible to go anywhere even on these relatively wide streets. I certainly got "trapped". Can you spot the different photography equipment aimed at the lion and count the number of each? Looks majority are mobile phone photographers. By the way, I forgot to say happy Chinese new year!

description:
万事如意, 新年快乐

Great Britain, London, Chinatown / Soho, 
Gerrard Street in London’s Soho is in the heart of Chinatown, it is in walking distance from Leicester Square, Piccadilly Circus & aprox. 500 mtr from Trafalgar Square. The Chinese community firmly established on Gerrard Street by the late 1960s, however, Chinatown goes back as far as the 18th century when it was in the East End. 
The London Chinatown has its own style, people feel displaced to Asia. A big gate marks the entrance to the Chinatowns Gerrad Street, here begins a pedestrian filled with red lanterns, dragons, Mandarin street signs & chock-a-block full of Chinese restaurants. The shops & eateries on the Gerrad Street are due to the tourists slightly more expensive than in the rest of Chinatown. It is particularly busy during the weekend, when the local Chinese community goes shopping for authentic Asian foodstuffs, not readily available elsewhere.
The most colourful time to visit Gerrard Street is during the Chinese New Year, at the end of January/early February, 2014 the year of the wooden horse is on January 31th, besides many activities, huge papier-mâché dragons dance through the streets with fire-crackers banging on the pavement.
The London Chinatown today occupies only a fraction of their former extent. During the ‘20s, Kate Meyrick opened up the notorious Club 43 on Gerrard Street, a decadent nightclub hosting outrageous parties frequented by the rich & famous. Famed London jazz institution Ronnie Scott’s first opened up at # 39 Gerrard Street in 1959, but for fans of rock music is London Chinatown incidentally also a term, a basement in Gerrard Street was the location of the first rehearsal of Led Zeppelin in August 1968, the well-known song "Train kept a rolling " was allegedly written here. Due to the many clubs existing in Soho in the past, it is considered as the birthplace of British Rock ’n’ Roll.

...Danke, Xièxie 谢谢, Thanks, Gracias, Merci, Grazie, Obrigado, Arigatô, Dhanyavad, Chokrane to you &
 over 1.200.000 visits in my photostream with countless motivating comments

description: I haven't done a blended image for a while, always wanted to do this one but maybe its a bit extreme. Especially compared to - www.flickr.com/photos/simon__syon/8370405205/ or www.flickr.com/photos/simon__syon/9464515644/ It was done in the evening so light was tricky. My day or night ones come out ok but this combination of evening light and artificial lights when merged together really create extreme colour contrasts. ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ The name Chinatown has been used at different times to describe different places in London. The present Chinatown is part of the Soho area of the City of Westminster, occupying the area in and around Gerrard Street. description: 10th February 2013 fromTrafalgar Square to Rupert Street, London. Chinese New Year (annual free event), organised by London Chinatown Chinese Association. This was the first day of the New Lunar Year (the Year of the Snake). The Celebrations began with a Parade from Trafalgar Square to the edge of Chinatown (Rupert Street) via Charing Cross Road and Shaftesbury Avenue. By no means all those in the Parade were Chinese. There was everything from a South American Pan Pipe group to Morris dancers. Tribo London was founded in 2008 as an off-shoot of the Tribo Bloco of Bahia. Its members play Afro-Brazilian rhythms including Samba and Reggae at Carnivals and other events. More information: tribobandlondon.wordpress.com/.

title: Chinese New Year Celebrations (2013) 03 - Parade: Pandemonium Drummers
tags: greatbritain drums parade popularmusic chinesenewyearcelebrations pandemoniumdrummers
description: 10th February 2013 fromTrafalgar Square to Rupert Street, London. Chinese New Year (annual free event), organised by London Chinatown Chinese Association. This was the first day of the New Lunar Year (the Year of the Snake). The Celebrations began with a Parade from Trafalgar Square to the edge of Chinatown (Rupert Street) via Charing Cross Road and Shaftesbury Avenue. By no means all those in the Parade were Chinese. There was everything from a South American Pan Pipe group to Morris dancers. The Pandemonium Drummers first came together fro the Opening Ceremony of the London Olympics. They are now available for events wearing their Industrial Revolution costumes (Working Men and Women, a Brunel) from the Ceremony. More information: www.pandemoniumdrummers.co.uk/.

description:
2013 is the year of the black Snake. beginning on February 10th, shortly after the New moon in Aquarius.

The Snake is the sixth sign of the Chinese Zodiac, which consists of 12 Animal Signs.

People born in the Year of the Snake are reputed to be thoughtful and wise and approach problems rationally and logically, seldom instinctively.

The snake has a mixed reputation in China. It is associated with wisdom, beauty and intelligence but also pride and anger.  2013 is the year of the black Snake, beginning on February 10th, shortly after the New moon in Aquarius.

title: What a hat!
tags: camera england colour london hat happy candid joy trafalgarsquare happiness celebrations colourful happynewyear bloke streetshot yearofthedragon chinesenewyearcelebration 112picturesin2012 112in2012 11headwear
description: Although technically I think it might be a small cottage. Funny thing is, I think I saw this chap at the London Elephant Parade at Chelsea Hospital in 2010. And he was wearing a similarly outrageous item of homemade headwear incorporating laminated Elephant Parade photos he'd taken. Did I post a photo of him from then? Yes I did. If anything, he's turned the dial on his hat amplifier to at least 11 now (Spinal Tap reference, movie lovers). Funny - I caught something on the local news which seemed to be saying that this Summer (2012) there'll be art installations of decorated telephone boxes all over London. More info if/when I hear more. For theme #11 - "Headwear" of the group "112 pictures in 2012". IMG_48405, 30%, plain and simple

title: Symbol of good luck and Wisdom - Year of Dragon
tags: portrait london festival canon eos chinatown dragon chinesenewyear parade 2012 dragondance 500d yearofdragon
description:
The Dragon dance showcased during the Chinese New Year celebrations, China Town, London.

The Dragon is one of the 12-year cycle of animals which appear in the Chinese and Mongolian zodiac related to the Chinese and Mongolian calendar, and the only animal that is legendary. The Year of the Dragon is considered the luckiest year in the Chinese Zodiac.

Occupying the 5th position in the Chinese Zodiac, the Dragon is the mightiest of the signs. Dragons symbolize such character traits as dominance and ambition. Dragons prefer to live by their own rules and if left on their own, are usually successful. They’re driven, unafraid of challenges, and willing to take risks. They’re passionate in all they do and they do things in grand fashion.

Of the 5 elements such as Metal, Wood, Water, Fire, Earth, Year 2012 belongs to Water Dragon

ISO 100 | f/4 | L- 55-250mm | Info from Wikipedia

description:
I met Mier and Sukie on the Chinese equivalent to Hogmanay - or New Years Eve, when Chinatown was bustling.

They were trying to get someone to take a picture of them with their camera phone and I offered to upload an image to Flickr that may have better lighting etc for them to download.  Sadly it was so busy that the composition is a bit average but hopefully they will like the picture!

title: day 036:05/02/11
tags: new rabbit dance chinatown dragon year chinese cny project365 3652011
description: Happy Chinese New Year Although the actual date was a Thursday (which turned out to be a very busy workday), I visited London's Chinatown to see the New Year celebrations which run throughout the weekend for the purpose of catching the atmosphere and witnessing the dragon dance. No sooner than I got a good shot, I was told to move on as I was in the way of the dragon. It makes me wonder, if they do it all day, it must be truly exhausting for the people inside doing it for the best part of an eight hour day. Unless they change. London's Chinatown is a relative recent invention, only developing in the Sixties and Seventies after the migrations of people from Hong Kong. There was an older one in Limehouse but that was obliterated in the Blitz.

title: Moon Cake Festival in Gerrard Street, Chinatown, London
tags: city uk decorations england urban food london cooking cake festival shop evening baking chinatown sweet soho chinese stall celebrations bakery pastries w1 confectionery mooncake moonfestival confections gerrardstreet londonist mooncakefestival
description:
A stall selling mooncakes and other pastries in front of a bakery for the Moon Festival in Gerrard Street, Chinatown, London

The mid-autumn festival celebrates the harvest at the time of the autumn full moon with food offerings made in honour of the moon. Mooncakes are regarded as an indispensable part of the celebrations.

The festival is held on the 15th day of the eighth month in the Chinese calendar—essentially the night of a full moon—which is in September or early October in the Gregorian calendar.

title: Phot.London.China.Town.01.041327.5659.jpg
tags: frankartculinaryyahoode
description:
Great Britain, London, Chinatown, Wardour Street, leading to the Gerrard Street in London’s Soho is in the heart of Chinatown, it is in walking distance from Leicester Square, Piccadilly Circus & aprox. 500 mtr from Trafalgar Square. The Chinese community firmly established on Gerrard Street by the late 1960s, however, Chinatown goes back as far as the 18th century when it was in the East End. 

The London Chinatown has its own style, people feel displaced to Asia. A big gate marks the entrance to the Chinatowns Gerrad Street, here begins a pedestrian filled with red lanterns, dragons, Mandarin street signs & chock-a-block full of Chinese restaurants. The shops & eateries on the Gerrad Street are due to the tourists slightly more expensive than in the rest of Chinatown. It is particularly busy during the weekend, when the local Chinese community goes shopping for authentic Asian foodstuffs, not readily available elsewhere.

The most colourful time to visit Gerrard Street is during the Chinese New Year, at the end of January/early February, 2014 the year of the wooden horse is on January 31th, besides many activities, huge papier-mâché dragons dance through the streets with fire-crackers banging on the pavement.

The London Chinatown today occupies only a fraction of their former extent. During the ‘20s, Kate Meyrick opened up the notorious Club 43 on Gerrard Street, a decadent nightclub hosting outrageous parties frequented by the rich & famous. Famed London jazz institution Ronnie Scott’s first opened up at # 39 Gerrard Street in 1959, but for fans of rock music is London Chinatown incidentally also a term, a basement in Gerrard Street was the location of the first rehearsal of Led Zeppelin in August 1968, the well-known song "Train kept a rolling " was allegedly written here. Due to the many clubs existing in Soho in the past, it is considered as the birthplace of British Rock ’n’ Roll.

...Danke, Xièxie 谢谢, Thanks, Gracias, Merci, Grazie, Obrigado, Arigatô, Dhanyavad, Chokrane to you for over 
2.300.000 visits in my photostream with countless motivating comments

title: Fancy a Cuppa?
tags: jencafe teahouse restaurant dining tea cafe street dark night chinatown urban citystreets colorful color evening fb canoneos70d canon eos 70d dslr citylife cityliving city travel wanderlust tourism traveling touristattraction london england uk unitedkingdom greatbritain gb vacation klingon65 english british europe european nightphotography urbanphotography travelphotography nightlife nitelife shopping store corner green chinese asian
description:
With it's vivid jade green hue, Jen Cafe is very easy to find. Their specialty is tea – and lots of it. From the floral sweet Chrysanthemum Honey to fruity Lemon Kumquat, most of their Asian teas can be served hot or cold to suit your mood – or temperature. Bubble teas of all colors, flavors and sweetness can also be enjoyed at Jen Café.
Outside of this, there’s enough variety on their menu to give many of Chinatown’s restaurants a run for their money. Their barbecue options are especially plentiful.

description:
(English Below)
'Pues parece que hace fresco, o que?!'
Se ha levantado el fresco, si, ya era hora, que estamos casi en Diciembre y aun iba con chaqueta!! 
Ayer, con esos -2 nos fuimos a China Town a comer a un restaurante, bien rico todo, y mas tarde a un bar que venden chupitos con ajo.... si: Garlic n' Shots.... me pedi una cerveza y tal.
Bueno, al grano, básicamente he probado EL objetivo, el 50mm f 1.4 de canon, que maravilla mecagoenlalechemerche. Tengo el 1.8 pero es que le he cogido mania a este tastarro, no se por que, la verdad es que andaba un poco mosca, por que tirar a 1.4 son palabras mayores, pero me ha sorprendido su enfoque suave, rapidez durante la noche y que es ligero, es el compi perfecto creo yo. Supongo que en cuanto pueda se unirá a la familia. 

---------------------------------
'Little bit chilly eh?!'
Finally it's cold, we've been waiting almost December but finally it's cold!
Yesterday we went to China Town to a restaurant and later on to a bar called Garlic n' Shots, I just asked for a beer at this one, really didn't trust that garlic thing, hahahaha.
One of my work mates lend me his 50mm f 1.4 which I loved, I have the 1.8 though but... I don't know but it's not my cup of tea, can't get well with it. 
I was quite scared to shoot with a 1.4 but I loved it, it's soft, tiny and quick even during night shoots. I'll buy it when I can, soon I guess, hahahahah.

title: Charing Cross library
tags: london library charingcross
description:
Noticed today that one of the banners says library, but the other has a series of chinese characters.
Luckily I have a colleague who speaks chinese, who confirms it says Charing Cross Library - literally, the first character has the same sound as the chinese word for tea (Chah).
She also told me how to say the word for library: Túshūguăn

ps I guess this is because its situated just around the corner from Chinatown?

tags: london city westminster chinatownlondon chinatown soho gerrard street china community architecture jencafe tea specialist restaurant cafe jen イギリス ロンドン 建築 建物 ニコン nikon d60 ソーホー チャイナタウン
description: Chinatown, Soho, City of Westminster, London, W1. Chinatown is the centre for the Chinese community in London and developed during the 1950s and 60s around Gerrard Street, a low rise Georgian, brick terraced street containing restaurants and shops. The community relocated to Soho from the original Chinatown in Limehouse in the Docklands following the decline of the shipping industry and the Docklands as a centre for international trade. www.chinatownlondon.org/index

title: One Sided (Soho Noir Shoot With Estrany), London Soho
tags: light shadow portrait blur colour eye closeup contrast one noir dof flash soho artificial headshot contact shallow strobist estrany yahoo:yourpictures=closeup
description:
This is actually a test shot, taken whilst setting up lights on a shoot with Estrany for my Soho Noir series.

Whilst incomplete, the lighting is way bolder than I'd usually shoot - and I really like how the edgy light combines with Estrany's hard, almost murderous stare.  

The processing is incomplete too - the full process, taking out her mole, etc. was just too porcelain to keep the edge.  This version feels right to me, with the human imperfections retained, but so penetrating a stare and so intimate a frame it is amplified.

Estrany is great; she's on Purpleport, superb to work with and if you're in London you should give her a shout!

Hope everyone is having an incredible week!

Info for Strobist:

Canon 600EX-RT in 38" softbox camera left, at 1/64 power and around 1m from Estrany, slightly up.  Triggered with RT from non-firing on camera Canon600EX-RT, used for focus assist only.

title: A Dance to the Music of Time, 1634
tags: 091021 london cityofwestminster nationalgallery museum nicolaspoussin poussinandthedance poussin dance composition choreographer adancetothemusicoftime time lyre poverty labour wealth pleasure putti apollo dawn hours giuliorospigliosi popeclementix
description:
Poussin

Four allegorical figures join hands and dance to the music of Time's lyre. 
Their rhythmic roundel emphasises how the cycle of life circles from one state to the next.  Poverty, barefoot in green with his back to us, leads to Labour in a simple orange gown.  She, in turn, brings Wealth, with pearls in her hair, shot-silk robe and golden sandals.  Following Wealth comes Pleasure, who smiles beguilingly at us beneath her floral crown, seemingly unaware that Poverty is just around the corner.  The fleeting nature of life is underscored by the putti who blow bubbles and hold an hourglass aloft.  Overhead, the sun god Apollo charges across the morning sky, preceded by Dawn scattering flowers and the Hours, who are also dancing. 
This vibrantly coloured, intellectually complex painting was commissioned from Poussin by the poet and prelate Giulio Rospigliosi (1600-1669), later Pope Clement IX.  Rospigliosi, who wrote several operas that featured dancing, seems to have collaborated with Poussin on the unusual subject matter, exploring both the aesthetic pleasures and allegorical possibilities of dance. 
[National Gallery]

Taken in the exhibition


Poussin and the Dance
(October 2021 – January 2022)

Tambourines shake, wine spills, and half-naked figures whirl across the canvas in these paintings of revelry, dance and drama that are brought together in this first exhibition dedicated to Poussin and dance.
Poussin’s paintings of dance are unique. He brings to life the classical world of gods and mortals with wild and riotous movement but, the chaos on the canvas does not reflect the meticulous and inventive process that allowed him to capture bodies in motion.
In this exhibition, Poussin’s paintings and drawings of dance will be shown alongside the antique sculpture he studied, inviting you to trace the evolution of his ideas from marble to paper to paint.
Poussin’s working methods will be fully revealed with a reconstruction of his wax figurines, which the artist manipulated into different compositions, underscoring his role not only as painter of dance, but as choreographer in his own right. 
[National Gallery]

title: BARTHOLOMEUS BRUYN THE ELDER
tags: england london nationalgallery bartholomeusbruyntheelder
description:
The Virgin, Saints & a Holy Woman, Ca.1530.

This panel was most probably the right-hand wing of a diptych (a painting made up of two panels), and was probably made in Cologne in the 1530s. The location of the left-hand panel is unknown, but it most probably showed the dead Christ.

The figures huddle together and look in the direction of the left panel, weeping at the sight. Their grief is dramatic – their faces anguished, their tears like glass beads on their cheeks. The rippling folds of their brightly coloured clothing intensify the emotion.

The woman at the front is the Virgin Mary, wearing her traditional blue mantle, which here has fur lining at the cuffs. The woman to her left is Mary Magdalene, identifiable by her long, loose hair. Like the holy woman wearing the cap behind her, Mary Magdalene is dressed in elaborate clothing more contemporary to the picture – her billowing sleeve is patterned with silk brocade. The man is Saint John the Evangelist, who is known as the ‘beloved disciple’ and to whom Christ, at the Crucifixion, entrusted his mother.

The National Gallery, London.

title: Peter Paul Rubens, Das Urteil des Paris - The Judgement of Paris
tags: uk greatbritain england nationalgallerylondon peterpaulrubens thejudgementofparis dasurteildesparis
description:
Peter Paul Rubens,  Siegen 1577 – Antwerpen 1640
Das Urteil des Paris - The Judgement of Paris (1632 – 35)
National Gallery London

Three goddesses stand before a shepherd. Mercury, the messenger god, has brought them to the young man and alighted behind him, his cloak still flying. The caduceus by which Mercury is recognised almost slips from his hand as he gazes at the goddesses. The shepherd is Paris, son of King Priam of Troy, abandoned as a baby because it was prophesied he would bring ruin to the city. He was brought up by shepherds but the gods had other plans for him.

Eris, goddess of discord, was the only immortal not invited to an important wedding. Furious at being left out, she threw a golden apple inscribed ‘To the Fairest’ among all the goddesses at the feast. Three claimed the title – Minerva, Juno and Venus. Jupiter, chief of the gods, declared that Paris should be the judge, and it is this moment of choice that Rubens has depicted.

In the version of the story by the Roman poet Lucian that Rubens used, Paris was unable to decide on a winner when the goddesses were clothed, so asked that they should disrobe. On the left, Minerva, goddess of war and wisdom, pulls her robe over her head. Her owl is perched on a tree above, her weapons are by her side. On the right, Juno, Jupiter’s wife, lifts her cloak to one side. At her feet, the peacock by which she is recognised reaches forward with a hiss at the sheepdog lying at the young shepherd’s feet. For Paris hands the golden apple to Venus, goddess of love, in the centre. She wears her tokens, pearls and roses, in her hair. Her little son, Cupid, god of erotic love, crouches on the ground behind her. Venus clutches her robe to her breast as if surprised she has won, but the goddesses had all cheated. Juno offered Paris wealth and power, Minerva offered wisdom and strength. Venus promised him the most beautiful woman in the world, Helen, who was married to King Menelaus of Sparta – an irresistible gift.

Rubens has turned the story into a rural idyll, with a far-reaching landscape set in the soft evening sun. The three nude figures each show a different aspect of the female form, painted with a sensitive, almost tender understanding of the body in various states of subtly conveyed emotion. The textures of silk, velvet, feathers and hair brushing against pale flesh glowing in the light are Rubens at his sensual best. Only a few grey clouds and the reflection of the gorgon Medusa with the snakes in her hair in Minerva’s mirror disturb the scene, suggesting that peace won’t last.

And Juno made sure there was no happy ending. Almost hidden in the clouds above the heads of the figures here is the implacable Fury, Alecto. In a jealous rage, Juno commanded her to wreak havoc on the Trojans. She caused Paris to abduct Helen, and the famous Trojan War began.

Source: National Gallery, London

description:
Portrait of Susanna Lunden ("Le Chapeau de paille")
probably 1622-1625
The sitter is thougth to be Susana Lunden, the daugther of the wealthy Antwerp silk merchant Daniel Fourment, and sister of Rubens second wife, Helena. Susanna wears a marriage or betrothal ring, and the painting probably dates from shortly after her marriage to Arnold Lunden en 1622.
oil on oak
Source: National Gallery

Retrato de Susanna Lunden ( " Le Chapeau de paille " )
probablemente 1622-1625
Se cree que se trata de Susana Lunden , la hija del acaudalado comerciante de seda de Amberes Daniel Fourment , y hermana de la segunda esposa de Rubens, Helena . Susanna usa un anillo de matrimonio o compromiso, y la pintura data probablemente de poco después de su matrimonio con Arnold Lunden en 1622 .
Pintura sobre madera 
Fuente: National Gallery

title: Thomas Gainsborough Mr and Mrs William Hallett ('The Morning Walk') / El señor y la Señora William Hallett "El paseo de la mañana"
tags: inglaterra portrait people london art museum artwork arte gente pareja retrato silk nationalgallery personas londres oil museo 1785 seda matrimonio pintura gainsborough oleo oiloncanvas hallett sxviii obradearte vestidodenovia themorningwalk vestidodeseda 18thcentry elpaseodelamañana
description:
'The Morning Walk' by Thomas Gainsborough shows an elegant young couple strolling through a woodland landscape, an attentive dog at the lady's heel. William Hallett and Elizabeth Stephen were both aged 21 and due to be married in the summer of 1785, shortly after the painting was completed.
William is in a black, silk velvet frock-suit. His apparent carelessness is actually a studied pose. The undone jacket and with one hand tucked into it is a stance seen in many fashionable 18th-centry informal portraits (known as conversation pieces). 'John Plampin', also by Gainsborough does the same.
Elizabeth is in a dress of ivory silk - perhaps her wedding dress - caught at the waist with a black silk band. A frilled muslin kerchief covers her breast, with a knot of grape-green ribbon under it.
The light, feathery brushstrokes used to describe the landscape are typical of Gainsborough's late style. William's hair and Elizabeth's gauzy shawl almost blend into the landscape they walk through.
Source: National Gallery

"El paseo de la mañana" por Thomas Gainsborough muestra una joven y elegante pareja paseando por un boscoso paisaje y  un perro atento a los pies de la señora. William Hallett y Elizabeth Stephen eran ambos de 21 años y se casaron en el verano de 1785, poco después de que se completó la pintura.
William está vestido con un traje de terciopelo de seda negro. Su aparente desenfado es en realidad una pose estudiada. La chaqueta desabrochada y con una mano metida en ella es una postura visto en muchos retratos informales de moda en el S.XVIII (conocidos como piezas de conversación).
Elizabeth lleva un vestido de seda color marfil - tal vez su vestido de novia - con la cintura ceñida con una cinta de seda negra. Un pañuelo de muselina con volantes cubre su pecho, con un nudo de cinta verde debajo del mismo.
Las pinceladas de luz, usadas para describir el paisaje son típicas del estilo tardío de Gainsborough. El pelo de William y el chal de gasa de Elizabeth casi se funden en el paisaje que atraviesan.
Fuente: National Gallery

title: Mr. & Mrs. Hallet 'The Morning Walk' 1785. Thomas Gainsborough (1727-1788)
description: Gainsborough portrayed William Hallett (1764-1842) and Elizabeth Stephen (1763-1833) shortly before their marriage on 30 July 1785. They may be wearing their wedding clothes. Mrs. Hallett's silk dress, the white fur of the Pomeranian sheepdog, and the foliage are evoked with the light brushstrokes Gainsborough used in the last phase of his career.

title: Moonfruit.com // www.you.com
description: Strong and simple was the overall design brief for Moonfruit.com's latest commercial. The internet based company that provides online platform solutions for business users wanted to showcase their growing customer base while also driving traffic to their site hub. The idea was to use real business users as the URL sandwiched between very specifically designed www. / .com bookends, for instance the bike shop text was treated with a metallic, industrial feel whereas the ballet manufacturer was textured with silk. It worked really well; the nicely designed text sat either side of the main protagonist, tying the two together in a very visual way. description:
Antonis van Dyck (Antwerpen 1599 - London 1641)
William Feilding, 1st Earl of Denbigh (1633 - 34)
National Gallery, London

William Feilding (about 1582 - 1643), created Earl of Denbigh in 1622, was the brother-in-law of the royal favourite George Villiers (created Duke of Buckingham in 1623), and held positions at court under James I and Charles I. He died from wounds received while fighting for Charles I in the Civil War.

From 1631-33 he visited Persia and India; the picture was probably painted soon after his return, and in commemoration of his journey. The Earl wears a silk Hindu or Indian jacket and pyjamas, and is shown hunting with an oriental servant, who points out a parrot in a palm tree.

Source: National Gallery, London

title: BABUSHKA - SNAPSHOT OF A KILLER (Chapter eight)
tags: street friends people london death hit nikon kill russia mark victim story revenge killer murder violence novel killed betrayal cleaner russian babushka russians tatiana assassin chapters violent russiandolls dmitri hitman bloodshed d700 paidassassin ilobsterit
description:
.

BABUSHKA - SNAPSHOT OF A KILLER (Chapter eight) 



.




Выражает сожаление по поводу, у меня есть, но мало

REGRETS, I HAVE BUT A FEW





.



 Near leicester Square, Central London, England.


(From the lips of an assassin)







.


 It was the cleaner all the time.

Right under my nose. Asian flesh, that glance upwards in my direction as I approached, standing opposite the Securicor armoured vehicle. Bucket and mop pressed studiously into service as he went about his daily chores seemingly oblivious to the street life that went gaily by him and my radar, normally so astute and refined, went sleepy-bye-bye just for a moment, a moment that was to cost me dear.

I should have seen him, I should have noticed the tell tale signs. Immersed in my own little world, a head full of thoughts and feelings, questions that needed answering, off the game, damn it, so amateurish.

After all, didn't they teach us that power is nothing without control? 

I remember growing weary of that very fact being drilled into us during training, though standing here today, I guess you could say I understand the sentiment more so than ever before. The sound of Big Ben chiming in the distance fills my ears and awakens me from my fruitless recollections as the pistol finally drops to my side and I unscrew the silencer which is hot to the touch, even through my gloved hand. My work here is done. A beautiful home in the centre of one of the world's most vibrant cities, family portraits adorning the walls like cherished memories of each laugh and footstep on the journey of these carefree lives.

 But the Reaper has come on the twisting winds of fate that have blown in so callously, claiming the lives, ending the story, snuffing the lights of existence and resigning them to the confines of loved ones memories from this moment on. . There is an irony here, a perverse parallel to that of my own childhood and the fear with which we all lived as the violence and mayhem of one man swept over us like a dark blanket that blocked out the light of love and reason. As I place my leather clad fingers onto the frame of one photograph, lifting it from it's mounting, I gaze at the smiling face of childhood that beams outwards, touching the lips with my index finger, lamenting the fact that I have never truly experienced the love of a mother or father who did not view life from the cellars of the abyss, from the bottom of a dirty Vodka bottle.



 A child craves unconditional love and attention, arms that wrap around her to ward away all evils, the knowing looks that tell her she is safe, the sanctity of touch. This child craved the violence and release that accompanied the slaughter of a brutal father, and since that day you'll forgive me if the milk of human kindness has less than flowed through this blackened heart of mine, these bleak arteries, this dark soul. Born of hatred and animosity, a childhood devoid of the basic foundation from which to grow good and strong, emotionally crippled, physically brutalised, here I stand the product of my parents shortcomings, the result of my rebellion and backlash.



 I replace the photograph and place my pistol back into it's holster, moving through dour pastel painted central hallway past the neatly trimmed potted plants and ornate marble flooring and the body of the ageing and loyal family butler who never saw the violent whirlwind coming that would make the newspapers the following morning. Someone's father, brother, granddad, retirement age and living this life of humble servitude to make ends meet. A victim of circumstances like us all, I guess. But you learn to never believe a word of secrecy uttered by lips that tremble with fear at the end of your trigger finger, and a dead witness cannot give you away, point you out in a packed court room or place you in a rotting cell with his testimony. Humanity dwindling like the sand grains in an hour glass, sentiment all but used up these days, no quarter, no mistakes, no mercy.

 I gather my cell phone and flip the cover, dialling the single speed dial number as the screen offers up the boxes for my confirmation codes, accompanied by photographs of the marks in sequence. Derek James, aged forty nine, lays slumped in front of his master bedroom mirror, patterned tie half fixed and a trickle of blood across his Gucci striped work shirt. Margaret, his wife of twenty something years, aged forty five, lays in bed, Grey silk nightdress fallen at the corner to reveal a milky white breast and morning hair, the look of shock registered on her lined face and remnants of the breakfast tray strew across the satin bedsheets. Freshly brewed coffee, a hint of Vanilla and Hazelnut in the air, croissants and strawberry conserve, thick wholemeal toast with a smattering of real butter and fresh grapefruit juice in crystal tumblers. A life of opulence and decadence that few would ever become accustomed to, lived in a world of lies and deceit behind closed doors and business trips explained by lips with a dual identity to a doting partner with a penchant for expensive clothes and shopping trips on the exclusive Platinum credit card.

 We play the roles and lip-synch to the soundtrack of life, hand on the bible at Sunday Communion spouting lies to a false prophet who little cares about our deceitful ways so long as we put money on the silver platter and repent for sins we'd take to the grave rather than give up. Shackled and chained to lives of conformity, dressed up in silk and lace, hidden by a thin veneer of personal possession. Is any man or woman truly free? 



Just for a moment my mind wanders and my heart sinks. Lies, I have told so many to people who have crossed my path. Does a tiny part of me, somewhere buried deep within the knees of my heart, the hidden shallows of my soul, yearn for the love of a good man, arms wrapped tenderly around my body and the trappings of a contented life away from all of the death and mayhem that I wreak on a global basis? 



Ageing is a given, something I have come to expect, but these changing feelings deep within are something new and frightening to one so bold and unshakable. Am I growing tired of life out of suitcases, the tedium of Hotel rooms and false identities as I skip borders and walk like a ghost through cities littered with the memories of those I have killed, am I envious of those couples hand in hand gazing into adoring eyes that tell them how much they are wanted, needed? 

Could someone such as I ever truly walk away from the business of death? Perhaps if circumstances were different I could have been a different person, but here I stand, surveying my handy work, covering my tracks, justifying my actions once more.



 Who am I kidding? 



For the second time, a mark who features in my recollections. The stiff Brit with the kind eyes who once I was introduced to by Dmitri before a hit in Cyprus. James was high ranking in the organization, a man of few words, all of them considered and reasoned. How did he turn bad? At what point did the lure of pastures green and plentiful lure him away from the comfort of what he already knew, what he already had? Hey, it's not my place to consider the logic, this blunt instrument of death is configured to kill, not think. But still the questions loom large in my head. 

My right thumb and index finger rub the gold surface of the wedding ring that I removed from Margaret's hand as a memento of the kill. We each have our ways I guess, our foibles. A lock of hair, a photograph at random, a piece of jewellery to mark the kill. Of course there are the extremes. Agents who snip the tops of the victim's index fingers are not uncommon, a slither of skin to cure and dry, an eye as a trophy seems extreme when glass presentation cases are perfectly suited to the beauty of butterfly collections. I kill on orders, I act on instinct, there is little time for compassion though respect for my mark is a prerequisite and the speed of the kill essential in minimizing the act unless the extremes of pain and suffering are a necessity of the assignment.



 Maybe I need to be reminded, for as sure as eggs are eggs, I know that one day I will have to pay for my sins, face the consequences of each and every life I have taken. Each reminder keeps me humble, thankful, perhaps even reminded of my own mortality. . And I have had my share of such barbarity. I scarcely dwell upon the darker assignments when my victim's last protracted hours on this planet were filled with depravity and pain in equal and unrelenting measures. Skinning a man for pursuance of revenge and punishment takes skill and training, with pain as the liberating drug and judgement your weapon and discretion. But tearing that same flesh to ascertain precise details is an art form in itself, as the blood flows and body defends it's assault with the tactics of self preservation, water to awaken the blacked out mind, drugs to prolong the inability to faint or sleep. Severed fingers carefully dissected at each individual joint, dislocated or broken bones, hairline or spiral fractures under the impact of metal textured weapons, feeding a man his own organs, chapters in the darkness of the book I call my life. 

Am I proud of my heritage, my legacy of death and destruction? You better believe it. I'm the best at what I do, my services in demand, my conscience muddied more so than entirely clear. Man, woman or infant, they are all the same to this cold bitch. A life is a life no matter the years on this beautiful Earth, no matter the creed or colour, the status or ego that dies at my hands.



 Memories flood my mind of the tiny infant slain in Milan, Italy not so many moons ago. Heir apparent to the throne, the Mafia kingdom, when severing the bloodline was a necessity and command. Not a flinch nor hesitation as those tiny limbs flailed, a babe in arms who would never grow to realize the power and corruption of the family into which it was born. I never reason, never question my orders. But in the dead of night, when the mind wanders freely and limbs toss and turn as the cold sweats take a hold of my body like raging tornado's, I see the faces of each and every mark that I have despatched during this long career of mine. And the nightmares haunt me. The sounds of waves and footsteps in the sand, images in my head that fill my senses, the bodies of the dead writhing on the incoming tide, the walking dead who groan with outstretched arms as they walk towards me. I guess a killer has a conscience of sorts after all, as we file each hit into the dark recesses of our minds. Once there was a choice, though now all I have are words and deeds.



 Walking down the corridor past the attractive bevelled mirror and antique flower vase that houses an array of peacock feathers and fern with a deft eye for display that had to have come from a woman's hands, I walk past Jamie's bedroom. Slumped at his computer, keyboard number lock light flashing it's green neon heart out, and the hands of the seventeen year old still poised over the keys. A matter of metres away in a pretty green pastel room with en suite facilities and posters of Justin Bieber looking like a toothy chipmunk adorning the walls, lies Melissa, fifteen, prone and pretty as a picture as she lays on top of her freshly made bed, hands clasped in front of her like a fallen angel. . Down the gliding semi circular staircase with marble steps and finely polished oak wood railings, my view opens up to the main lobby and front door where the housemaid lies slumped as she fell, to the single bullet that she never anticipated as she opened the adjoining door from the kitchen just minutes earlier. 



The bodyguards number four in total, a thousand pounds of dumb meat, rippling muscles and raw aggression now slain, one in the lobby, a second in the kitchen, another in the front garden and the last at the wheel of his bosses gleaming British racing green Jaguar XF parked out front, despatched first of all with my trusty knife to an unsuspecting carotid artery as he made a private phone call on the Blue tooth during work time to one of his whores. Naughty boy, could get sacked for far less.



 The kill complete, I enter the daylight from the confines of the property like a foolish nightclub reveller, drunk on a cocktail of alcohol and amphetamines, blinded by the sudden streaks of sunlight in my eyes, confused and dazed, failing to react to the situation before it's too late. They say that pride goes before a fall, and mine is surely dented as my normally pin sharp senses fail to spot that Asian operative opposite the Securicor van, fifty yards from my position as I make a calm and casual exit from the scene of the crime. 

Damn, that was an inspired choice of vehicle that had me fooled. Eyes surveying the van, the pedestrians as they scurried like little ants this way and that, to and fro between pointless positions in another bland and meaningless day at the office. As the sharp pain in my right thigh turns to a numb sensation, I see the rifle and the half opened drivers window on that damned vehicle and the wire just behind the Asian guy's right ear as he nods and smiles at the shooter, my limbs buckling as I begin to fall where I stand. Instinctively I grab my gun, sans silencer as two men approach from the my flanks, the world around me becoming disjointed and blurred. Like a bad acid trip, my head spins and before I know it, an expertly placed fist has connected with my defenceless jaw. 

Lights out, I'm done for. Careless bitch! How did I ever get so sloppy? 





.



Rewritten on August 1st 2011

 
Original story penned in August 2011

 Photograph taken on July 14th 2011 in Upper Saint Martins Lane, off Leicester Square in Central London, England.

" The painting, probably of the 1520s, is of a shape associated in Venice with votive offerings to the Virgin Mary or a patron saint. Here the warrior who approaches the Virgin and Child on his knees wears European armour, but the silk of his head-dress is from Islamic North Africa, and the trappings of his horse from Islamic Spain; the dagger and belt hanging on the wall are also of Islamic style. It has been conjectured that the painting commemorates the conversion of a Turk. " - - - - Actually you want to extra cool coincidence? I went to this Talk&Draw at the national gallery, hence spotting the eerie similarity, and at the same time there were approx' 5000 people outside in Trafalgar Sq protesting the controversial satirical cartoons that appeared in Denmark. Check my out-of-practice drawings.
